title: H5N1 HPAI detected in wild birds, Netherlands
summary: The Netherlands reported detection of highly pathogenic avian influenza A(H5N1) in wild birds (barnacle geese and mute swans) found dead near wetlands in the province of Friesland. Samples confirmed HPAI H5N1 clade 2.3.4.4b. Passive surveillance in surrounding poultry farms has been intensified.
raw_content: WOAH Immediate Notification: Highly pathogenic avian influenza A(H5N1) in wild birds, Netherlands. Species affected: Branta leucopsis, Cygnus olor. Location: Friesland province. 12 dead birds tested positive. Clade 2.3.4.4b confirmed by Wageningen Bioveterinary Research.
analysis: Detection of H5N1 clade 2.3.4.4b in migratory waterfowl in the Netherlands signals renewed HPAI activity along the East Atlantic Flyway. Friesland province is a critical staging area for migratory birds that transit through Central Europe. While direct risk to Switzerland is currently limited, the strain's presence in this region warrants enhanced passive surveillance in Swiss wetland areas. Historical patterns suggest southward spread within 2-4 weeks during late winter migration.

title: Dengue fever surge reported in Thailand, 50% increase year-on-year
summary: Thailand's Ministry of Public Health reported 28,500 dengue cases and 34 deaths for the first two months of 2026, a 50% increase compared to the same period in 2025. DENV-1 and DENV-2 are the predominant circulating serotypes. Bangkok, Chiang Mai, and southern provinces most affected.
raw_content: WHO Disease Outbreak News: Dengue fever — Thailand. As of 28 February 2026, the Ministry of Public Health reported a cumulative total of 28,500 dengue cases including 34 deaths (CFR 0.12%). This represents a 50% increase compared to the same period in 2025. Predominant serotypes: DENV-1 (58%), DENV-2 (31%).
analysis: The 50% year-on-year increase in Thailand indicates an intensifying dengue season, likely driven by above-average rainfall and expanded Aedes aegypti habitat. The co-circulation of DENV-1 and DENV-2 raises concerns about secondary infections with more severe outcomes. Swiss relevance is limited to returning travellers, but travel health advisories should be updated. Thailand remains a top destination for Swiss tourists during spring.

title: Cholera outbreak in Ethiopia — Oromia and Somali regions
summary: Ethiopia reported 523 confirmed cholera cases and 12 deaths in Oromia and Somali regions since mid-February 2026. The outbreak is linked to contaminated water sources following recent flooding. V. cholerae O1 Ogawa confirmed by national laboratory. WASH interventions and oral cholera vaccination campaigns underway.
raw_content: WHO Disease Outbreak News: Cholera — Ethiopia. As of 28 February 2026, a total of 523 confirmed cases of cholera and 12 deaths (CFR 2.3%) have been reported from Oromia and Somali regions. V. cholerae O1 serotype Ogawa confirmed. Flooding in January 2026 likely contributed to contamination of water sources.
analysis: The cholera outbreak in Ethiopia is consistent with seasonal flooding patterns and fragile WASH infrastructure in the affected regions. The case fatality rate of 2.3% is above the WHO target of <1%, indicating potential gaps in case management. International spread risk is low. Swiss relevance is minimal, limited to humanitarian response coordination.

title: Seasonal influenza activity increasing across Europe — week 9 update
summary: ECDC weekly influenza update reports increasing activity across Western Europe in week 9 of 2026, with Influenza A(H3N2) as the dominant circulating subtype. France, Germany, Italy, and Spain report medium-intensity activity. ICU admissions remain within expected seasonal range.
raw_content: ECDC Seasonal Influenza Report, Week 9/2026: Influenza activity increasing across Western Europe. Dominant subtype: A(H3N2). Medium intensity reported in FR, DE, IT, ES. Sentinel primary care consultation rates above baseline threshold in 18 EU/EEA countries. ICU admissions stable.

title: Foot-and-mouth disease confirmed in cattle, Algeria
summary: ProMED reports confirmed foot-and-mouth disease in cattle herds in northern Algeria (Blida province). FMD virus serotype O identified. Movement restrictions imposed in affected areas. 45 cattle affected across 3 farms with 3 deaths.
raw_content: ProMED-mail post: Foot-and-mouth disease — Algeria (Blida). FMDV serotype O confirmed in cattle. 45 animals affected, 3 deaths. Movement restrictions in place. First FMD report in Algeria since 2024.
